There was indication for immediate treatment as rupture of the diaphragm tend to increase in size with time rather than heal and the pressure gradient between the two compartment and also the movement of the diaphragm will continue to push the viscera in the chest with risk of complication such as for example obstruction ischemia or worsening respiratory distress.
Delayed diagnosis of diaphragmatic hernia in patients with hysteria trauma has been associated with a worse prognosis.
In this case, the clinical and radiological findings ruled out the need of a laparotomy.
The thoracoscopic approach could confirm diagnosis with 100% sensitivity and also treat with direct suture.
The contralateral pneumothorax was very small, but we thought it was sensible to put a right chest strain before starting selective ventilation on the right lung.
